title: 17-Hydroxyprogesterone in the Newborn: Pitfalls in Ruling Out or Diagnosing CAH
subtitle: A Fellow Training Module: five-coordinate interpretation (age at sampling, GA/BW, stress, glucocorticoid exposure, assay/specimen); false-positive and false-negative patterns; 17 published-case teaching set; diagnostic algorithm; EMS/NBS thresholds — do not use 17-OHP as a stand-alone rule-out
authors: Fellow training module — compiled 2026-07-03 (Dr. Ajay Kumar Shukla); refs: Gudmundsson 1999, Rohrer 2003, Houang 2022, Rizzuto 2026, Schreiner 2008, Held 2020, Endocrine Society 2018
sourceFile: CAH_17OHP_Newborn_Pitfalls_Fellow_Module.md; Speiser PW JCEM 2018; Held PK Int J Neonatal Screen 2020

relatedChapters.title: NCCAH: The Metabolic Trap & Classic CAH Reclassification

patient.chiefComplaint: Newborn 17-OHP screening and confirmatory CAH work-up — pitfalls module
pmh: Case themes: borderline early 17-OHP with virilization (Gudmundsson); dexamethasone false-negative NBS (Rohrer); maternal triamcinolone (Houang); four negative NBS in preterm/LBW (Rizzuto); screening-negative later virilization (Onuma); normal 17-OHP with 11β-OHD (Singer-Granick); high 17-OHP with 3β-HSD2 (Levy-Shraga, Jeandron); PORD mimics (Fukami, Wudy); EDTA false-positive DBS (Fingerhut); transient hyper-17-OHPemia (Mizuno, Cavarzere)

caseDescription: Fellow deep-dive on 17-hydroxyprogesterone (17-OHP) in newborn screening and confirmatory diagnosis of congenital adrenal hyperplasia. Core principle: 17-OHP is a screening analyte, not a standalone diagnosis. The commonest pitfall is interpreting a single value without five coordinates — age at sampling after birth, gestational age/birth weight, clinical stress/NICU context, glucocorticoid exposure (antenatal, maternal inhaled/nasal/topical, neonatal dexamethasone/hydrocortisone), and assay/specimen type (DBS immunoassay vs LC-MS/MS serum; native heel-prick vs EDTA-contaminated spots). False positives: early sampling (<24–48 h), preterm/LBW/stress, immunoassay cross-reactivity. False negatives: steroid suppression, mild/SV phenotypes, preterm physiology, non-21-OHD CAH. Seventeen published cases illustrate missed and misclassified infants. Includes permutation matrix, diagnostic algorithm, do/don't table, and board-style MCQs.
keyFindings: Anchor every 17-OHP to age at collection, GA/BW, illness/stress, steroid exposure, and assay/units
Most false positives: DBS before 24–48 h, preterm/LBW, stressed term infant, immunoassay cross-reactivity
Most dangerous false negatives: glucocorticoid exposure, borderline/SV disease, serial negative NBS in preterm with electrolyte abnormalities
Normal newborn screen never rules out CAH when genital findings, salt-wasting symptoms, family history, or hyponatremia/hyperkalemia present
Elevated 17-OHP ≠ 21-OHD alone — evaluate 3β-HSD2, 11β-OHD, PORD with broader steroid profile
Fasting not required for newborn DBS; time since birth matters more than clock time in first month
In adrenal crisis: treat first — do not delay hydrocortisone for a perfect panel
finalDiagnosis: Teaching module — not a single patient. Synthesis: treat 17-OHP as a conditional screening test whose meaning depends on pre-analytical and clinical context. When phenotype and biochemistry disagree, believe the baby and broaden the work-up (LC-MS/MS steroid profile, electrolytes/renin, ACTH stimulation, genetics). After infancy, monitoring uses consistently timed pre-dose samples — not NBS cutoffs.
learningObjectives: Apply the five-coordinate framework to every newborn 17-OHP result
Classify scenarios as false-positive risk, false-negative risk, or emergency
Recognize published pitfalls: steroid exposure, preterm NBS, EDTA contamination, rare CAH subtypes
Execute the diagnostic algorithm for abnormal screen or clinical suspicion
Distinguish newborn screening interpretation from post-infancy treatment monitoring

items.title: Why a single 17-OHP value is the commonest CAH newborn pitfall
items.content:
The commonest practical pitfall is interpreting a single 17-OHP without anchoring it to five coordinates: (1) age at sampling after birth — especially specimens collected too early; (2) gestational age and birth weight — especially preterm/LBW; (3) clinical stress/illness and NICU context; (4) glucocorticoid exposure — antenatal betamethasone/dexamethasone, maternal chronic/inhaled/nasal steroids, neonatal dexamethasone/hydrocortisone before the sample; (5) assay and specimen type — first-tier DBS immunoassay vs LC-MS/MS; native heel-prick DBS vs EDTA-contaminated spots.

17-OHP is a screening analyte, not a standalone diagnosis. In a sick newborn, adrenal crisis treatment must not wait for a perfect endocrine work-up.
keyPoints: Five coordinates — never a lone number.
Crisis → treat while sampling if safe.

items.title: How early sampling, prematurity, and stress produce false-positive 17-OHP screens
items.content:
Most common false-positive pattern: first-tier immunoassay 17-OHP elevated in a baby without classic 21-hydroxylase deficiency because the sample was collected before 24–48 h, or because the infant is preterm/LBW/stressed, with additional immunoassay cross-reactivity from fetal-zone steroid sulfates.

Healthy newborns have high 17-OHP at birth that falls over 24–72 h. Preterm infants have persistent fetal-zone physiology and immature enzyme activity — precursors can be high without CAH. Sick/stressed term infants: ACTH/stress elevates precursors — do not dismiss a high result as 'just stress' without electrolytes, renin, and clinical exam.

Teaching pearl: term vs post-term is not the major axis; preterm/LBW and timing after birth are.
keyPoints: Preterm/LBW — GA/BW-adjusted cutoffs; repeat at 48–72 h and ~28 d.

items.title: Why a normal newborn 17-OHP screen does not rule out CAH
items.content:
Most dangerous false-negative pattern: a baby with true CAH has a 'normal' screen because 17-OHP was not yet high enough, cutoffs were too high for the phenotype, the baby was preterm, or maternal/neonatal glucocorticoids suppressed the fetal/neonatal HPA axis.

Therefore: a normal newborn screen never rules out CAH when genital findings, salt-wasting symptoms, family history, or unexplained hyponatremia/hyperkalemia are present. Screening sensitivity is high but not 100% (Schreiner: 5/214 classic CAH missed). NBS mainly targets 21-OHD; 11β-OHD, 3β-HSD2, and PORD may present with atypical or normal 17-OHP patterns.
keyPoints: Steroids, early/mild disease, preterm → false negative.
Normal NBS + electrolytes abnormal → work up.

items.title: How assay type, units, and EDTA contamination mislead 17-OHP interpretation
items.content:
First-tier NBS generally measures 17-OHP in dried blood spots by immunoassay; LC-MS/MS second-tier improves specificity (21-deoxycortisol, androstenedione, cortisol ratios). Pitfalls: immunoassay cross-reactivity with 17-hydroxypregnenolone sulfate; comparing DBS ng/mL with serum ng/dL (100-fold unit mismatch); applying adult/term cutoffs to preterm DBS.

EDTA contamination of DBS causes false-positive 17-OHP on lanthanide fluorescence immunoassays (Fingerhut: 27 contaminated samples). Do not spot anticoagulated EDTA blood onto NBS cards unless the program explicitly accepts it. Confirmatory work-up: serum LC-MS/MS with cortisol, androstenedione, renin/electrolytes, broader steroid profile if borderline or atypical phenotype.

items.title: Case 1: Severe virilization with borderline early 17-OHP (Gudmundsson 1999)
items.content:
Newborn female with severe virilization yet newborn-screen and initial confirmatory 17-OHP only borderline. Authors emphasized timing, phenotype-biochemistry mismatch, and assay specificity.

Pitfall: borderline 17-OHP does not trump a convincing clinical phenotype. In a virilized 46,XX newborn, do not discharge the diagnosis because the first 17-OHP is borderline. Repeat serum LC-MS/MS, electrolytes, renin, and/or ACTH-stimulated steroid profile. Ref: J Pediatr Endocrinol Metab 1999;12(6):895-902.

items.title: Case 2: Prenatal/neonatal dexamethasone and negative screen with salt-wasting crisis (Rohrer 2003)
items.content:
Older sister had CAH → prenatal dexamethasone. Affected male fetus identified; dexamethasone stopped. Infant born preterm, received dexamethasone during mechanical ventilation, had unsuspicious neonatal screen, developed salt-wasting crisis at ~3 weeks.

Pitfall: steroid exposure can suppress 17-OHP enough for false-negative NBS. Family history or prenatal diagnosis should override screening — draw diagnostic labs and treat proactively. Ref: Biol Neonate 2003;83(1):65-68.
keyPoints: Salt wasting at 3 weeks despite normal screen.

items.title: Case 3: Maternal nasal triamcinolone and day-3 normal 17-OHP (Houang 2022)
items.content:
Newborn with family CAH risk: day-3 DBS 17-OHP below cutoff. LC-MS/MS on same DBS found elevated 21-deoxycortisol and maternal triamcinolone peak. By day 13, serum 17-OHP clearly high; CAH confirmed genetically.

Pitfall: 'no systemic steroid' may be false — chronic maternal nasal/inhaled steroids matter. Explicitly ask about antenatal, inhaled, nasal, topical, and postnatal steroid exposures. 21-deoxycortisol more specific for 21-OHD. Ref: Endocr Connect 2022;11(6):e220101.
keyPoints: High-risk family → test despite normal day-3.

items.title: Case 4: Four consecutive negative NBS in 35-week 1470-g infant (Rizzuto 2026)
items.content:
35-week male, 1470 g: four NBS samples days 2–38 all normal. Hyponatremia/hyperkalemia by day 26. Later LC-MS/MS serum 17-OHP >10,900 ng/dL; CYP21A2 deletion plus I172N.

Pitfall: serial negative NBS does not rule out CAH in preterm/LBW with persistent electrolyte abnormalities. Persistent hyponatremia/hyperkalemia in a newborn is an endocrine diagnosis until proven otherwise. Ref: PubMed 41797552.

items.title: How elevated 17-OHP can mislabel 3β-HSD2, 11β-OHD, or PORD as 21-OHD
items.content:
Elevated 17-OHP is not synonymous with 21-OHD. Case examples: Levy-Shraga — 46,XX phenotypic female, high NBS 17-OHP, no virilization, HSD3B2 classic 3β-HSD deficiency; Jeandron — positive screen, salt wasting day 13, CYP21A2 negative, HSD3B2 Q334X; Singer-Granick — normal neonatal 17-OHP, later 11β-hydroxylase deficiency with elevated 11-deoxycortisol/DOC; Fukami/Wudy — P450 oxidoreductase deficiency mimicking 21-OHD/aromatase deficiency with variable 17-OHP.

If phenotype is atypical: send 21-deoxycortisol, 11-deoxycortisol, DOC, 17-OH-pregnenolone, DHEA, androstenedione; consider urine GC-MS and CYP21A2/HSD3B2/CYP11B1/POR genetics. Positive 17-OHP + negative CYP21 can still be CAH.
keyPoints: 11β-OHD and PORD may have normal NBS 17-OHP.

items.title: Why some infants have transient hyper-17-OHPemia without CAH
items.content:
Mizuno 2004: preterm infant with persistent serum 17-OHP until 2 months — no CYP21A2 mutation; normalized by 3 months without treatment. Cavarzere 2009: NBS-positive subgroup with hyper-17-OHPemia without clinical signs or CYP21A2 mutations, normalizing by 3 months.

Pitfall: persistent mild/moderate elevation in an asymptomatic infant is not automatically classic CAH. Use trend, electrolytes, renin, exam, ACTH stimulation, and genotype before long-term steroids. Do not overtreat if data do not support CAH.
keyPoints: Not all NBS positives need lifelong steroids.

items.title: How to apply the newborn CAH diagnostic algorithm
items.content:
Abnormal 17-OHP screen or clinical suspicion:

If ill (vomiting, lethargy, dehydration, shock, hypoglycemia, hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, ambiguous genitalia) → draw critical samples if this does not delay care (glucose, Na/K, serum 17-OHP, cortisol, ACTH, renin/aldosterone, androstenedione ± testosterone, 11-deoxycortisol, DOC) → stress-dose hydrocortisone + saline/dextrose; call endocrinology.

If well: classify screen by sample age, GA/BW, NICU status, steroid exposure, assay. Very high 17-OHP + compatible phenotype → serum LC-MS/MS + electrolytes/renin. Borderline → repeat per program; LC-MS/MS second tier; ACTH stimulation if stable and unclear. High 17-OHP but atypical phenotype → broad steroid profile + genetics. Normal screen but clinical suspicion → normal NBS does NOT rule out CAH — diagnostic serum work-up.
keyPoints: Normal NBS + suspicion → serum profile anyway.

items.title: Why newborn screening rules differ from post-infancy 17-OHP monitoring
items.content:
Fasting is not required for newborn DBS CAH screening — do not delay heel-prick for fasting. In the first month, time since birth matters more than clock time (Sólyom: no reliable diurnal rhythm in untreated CAH neonates aged 2–26 days). By ~3 months, morning 17-OHP tends highest — collect consistently pre-dose before hydrocortisone for monitoring.

Bode 1999: home filter-paper monitoring captured undertreatment missed by random clinic timing. A single random clinic 17-OHP can falsely reassure. NBS cutoffs do not apply to known CAH monitoring — use assay-specific pre-dose profiles plus growth, BP, androstenedione/testosterone, renin.
keyPoints: Newborn: no fasting; age since birth > clock time.
Don't chase complete 17-OHP normalization — overtreatment harms growth.

items.question: Why must clinicians investigate CAH despite a normal newborn 17-OHP screen when the infant has hyponatremia and hyperkalemia?
items.answer: Because screening sensitivity is not 100%, preterm/LBW physiology and glucocorticoid exposure can produce false-negative results, and salt-wasting classic 21-OHD can evolve before or despite NBS reporting. Electrolyte abnormalities in a newborn represent mineralocorticoid deficiency until proven otherwise — the Rizzuto case had four normal NBS samples before crisis. Clinical status and electrolytes trump a normal screen.

items.question: How should a well term newborn with 17-OHP collected at 10 hours and normal genital exam be managed?
items.answer: Do not diagnose classic CAH or dismiss permanently. Early collection (<24 h) is a major false-positive pitfall because physiologic birth-surge 17-OHP is high. Repeat screen/confirm per program at appropriate age (typically 24–48 h or program-defined window), document GA/BW and illness, and reassess if family history or later symptoms emerge. Obtain serum LC-MS/MS and electrolytes if repeat remains elevated or infant becomes unwell.
keyPoints: 10 h sample → repeat at program age.
Do not start lifelong steroids on one early DBS.

items.question: Why does a positive 17-OHP screen in a 46,XX newborn without virilization require broadening the diagnostic work-up beyond CYP21A2 alone?
items.answer: Because 3β-HSD2 deficiency and other enzyme defects can elevate 17-OHP through peripheral conversion while producing an atypical phenotype — Levy-Shraga and Jeandron cases had high 17-OHP without expected virilization and negative or inconsistent CYP21A2 results. Labeling automatically as 21-OHD risks wrong treatment and missed salt-wasting from another CAH subtype. Full steroid profiling and HSD3B2/CYP11B1/POR testing are indicated.

items.question: How should critical samples be obtained in a newborn with suspected adrenal crisis and a pending 17-OHP result?
items.answer: Draw glucose, sodium, potassium, bicarbonate, serum 17-OHP, cortisol, ACTH, renin/aldosterone, and androstenedione if this does not delay care — but start stress-dose hydrocortisone and volume resuscitation immediately when crisis is suspected. A perfect pre-treatment panel must never delay life-saving steroids. Repeat confirmatory LC-MS/MS and genetics can follow stabilization.
keyPoints: Do not delay hydrocortisone for labs.

items.stem: A 10-hour-old well term newborn has elevated DBS 17-OHP. Genital exam is normal, electrolytes are normal. Best next step?
options: Diagnose classic CAH and start lifelong dexamethasone
Repeat screen/confirm per program at appropriate age and assess clinical risk
Require 8 hours fasting before any repeat sample
items.explanation: Early collection (<24 h) is a major false-positive pitfall; repeat at program-defined timing.

items.stem: A 35-week, 1.4-kg male has four normal NBS samples but persistent hyponatremia and hyperkalemia. Best interpretation?
options: Obtain serum LC-MS/MS 17-OHP/steroid profile and treat if clinically indicated
items.explanation: Rizzuto case — serial negative NBS in preterm/LBW with electrolyte crisis; believe labs over screen.

items.stem: A positive 17-OHP screen in a 46,XX newborn without virilization is repeated and remains high. CYP21A2 testing is inconsistent with 21-OHD. Next test?
options: HSD3B2/POR/CYP11B1 evaluation and full steroid profile
items.explanation: 3β-HSD2, 11β-OHD, and PORD can elevate 17-OHP — broaden beyond CYP21A2.

items.stem: Which statement about fasting and newborn CAH screening is correct?
options: Newborns must fast 8 hours before CAH screening
Non-fasting status causes false-positive 17-OHP on DBS
Fasting is not required for newborn DBS CAH screening
Formula feeding suppresses 17-OHP and invalidates the screen
items.explanation: Do not delay NBS for fasting — feeding status is not a meaningful screening variable in newborns.

items.stem: A mother used chronic nasal triamcinolone in pregnancy. The baby has family CAH history and a normal day-3 17-OHP screen. Best action?
options: Reassure — CAH impossible with normal screen
items.explanation: Houang case — maternal inhaled/nasal steroids can produce false-negative screens in high-risk infants.

items.stem: Which analyte is more specific for 21-OHD and less elevated in prematurity/other CAH forms?
items.explanation: 21-deoxycortisol is more specific for 21-OHD on second-tier LC-MS/MS profiling (Miller 2019; Wisconsin algorithm).

items.stem: A preterm infant's mildly elevated 17-OHP normalizes by 3 months. Electrolytes and renin are normal; ACTH stimulation and genotype do not support CAH. Best diagnosis?
items.explanation: Mizuno/Cavarzere — transient elevation without CAH; avoid unnecessary long-term steroids.

items.stem: What sample error can falsely elevate 17-OHP in some DBS immunoassays?
options: EDTA contamination of the dried blood spot
items.explanation: Fingerhut — EDTA interferes with lanthanide fluorescence immunoassays; spot native heel-prick blood.

items.stem: A stable infant has borderline 17-OHP and LC-MS/MS is unavailable. Diagnosis remains unclear. Most appropriate next step?
options: ACTH stimulation with at least cortisol and 17-OHP, ideally full steroid profile
items.explanation: ACTH stimulation clarifies borderline cases when second-tier LC-MS/MS unavailable and baby stable.

items.stem: For treatment monitoring in known CAH after infancy, which sampling strategy is least misleading?
options: Random afternoon sample without noting hydrocortisone dose time
Consistently timed early-morning pre-dose sample, or serial pre-dose profile
items.explanation: Bode/Sólyom — pre-dose morning timing matters for monitoring; diurnal variation emerges after infancy.

items.stem: Which factor is NOT one of the five coordinates required to interpret a newborn 17-OHP result?
items.explanation: Five coordinates: age at sampling, GA/BW, stress/illness, steroid exposure, assay/specimen type.

items.stem: Schreiner et al. reported that among 214 children with classical CAH, how many were not detected by neonatal 17-OHP screening?
items.explanation: Five missed cases (three simple-virilizing, two salt-wasting) — screening is not 100% sensitive.

items.statement: A normal newborn 17-OHP screen rules out CAH in an infant with ambiguous genitalia and hyponatremia.
items.explanation: False — most dangerous false-negative pattern; clinical findings override normal NBS.

items.statement: Newborns must fast before heel-prick CAH screening on dried blood spots.
items.explanation: False — do not delay NBS for fasting; feeding status does not drive neonatal interpretation.

items.statement: Emergency hydrocortisone for suspected neonatal adrenal crisis should be delayed until all confirmatory steroid labs are obtained.
items.explanation: False — treat crisis first; sample concurrently only if it does not delay care.
False — 3β-HSD2, 11β-OHD, and PORD can alter 17-OHP; phenotype may be atypical.

items.statement: In untreated CAH neonates under one month of age, time since birth matters more than time of day for 17-OHP interpretation.
items.explanation: True — Sólyom: no characteristic diurnal pattern in first weeks; clock time less important than sampling age.

items.assertion: A virilized 46,XX newborn with borderline initial 17-OHP requires repeat serum LC-MS/MS and further adrenal evaluation.
items.reason: Borderline early 17-OHP does not exclude classic CAH when the clinical phenotype is convincing, as timing and assay specificity can mask true disease (Gudmundsson case).
items.explanation: Both true — phenotype-biochemistry mismatch demands broader work-up.

items.assertion: Neonatal dexamethasone exposure can produce a false-negative CAH newborn screen.
items.reason: Glucocorticoids suppress ACTH and adrenal precursor accumulation, lowering 17-OHP below screening cutoffs despite true 21-hydroxylase deficiency.
items.explanation: Both true — Rohrer case and Houang maternal steroid cases illustrate this mechanism.
